text.title: Jira via Atlassian Rovo MCP, Permission-Inheriting Failure Modes
text.summary: 3 antipatterns observed in real deployments of this methodology. Each pairs symptom + root cause + concrete fix.

antipattern.symptom: An AI client bypasses Rovo MCP and calls Jira REST directly via a service-account token. Permission inheritance lost; audit trail wrong identity.
antipattern.root-cause: Service-account token issued to AI clients.
antipattern.fix: Revoke direct Jira tokens from AI clients; route every call through Rovo MCP.

antipattern.symptom: Two parallel MCP installations (one official, one a developer's experiment). Audit log fragmented.
antipattern.fix: Enforce a single Rovo MCP URL via DNS allowlist; reject all others at the network layer.

antipattern.symptom: MCP queries traverse outside the inviting user's projects. Confidential project data exposed.
antipattern.fix: Restrict scope_projects at the MCP config level; reject any query referencing outside-scope projects.
